science_subject.key_measurement: O–H bond length ~0.958 Å; H–O–H angle ~104.5° (NIST Chemistry WebBook, H₂O)
science_subject.visualization_prompt: Scientific chemistry visualization, water molecule H2O with shared electron pair density between oxygen and two hydrogens, bent geometry ~104.5 degrees, partial negative on oxygen and partial positive on hydrogens, ball-and-stick with transparent electron cloud lobes, neutral dark background, accurate relative angles, NOT TO SCALE on electron cloud exaggeration, 16:9 documentary fidelity

config.voice_suffix: clear enthusiastic science communicator voice, precise but accessible diction, synthetic presenter only

shots.video_prompt: CONTINUITY LOCK @Julian-001: identical presenter face, smart-casual wardrobe, seamless neutral set, same eye-line to camera — seamless continuation of prior take, zero jump cut. CONTINUITY LOCK @Set-Seamless-Neutral-001: identical neutral grey seamless — same backdrop tone mid-grey #9A9A98, same floor sweep, same subject-to-backdrop distance, same falloff — seamless continuation, zero jump cut. STYLE LOCK @Style-Cool-Clinical-001: cool-clinical grade — neutral white balance, low saturation, accurate color reproduction, minimal stylistic grading, science and product trust, no warm cozy cast, no film grain. Presenter addresses camera on seamless neutral, crisp science hook. Camera: locked medium, direct address. Neutral seamless 5500K bilateral soft key locked — symmetrical wrap, subtle nose shadow only, zero hue drift, no magenta vignette, no warm/cool lateral split. Neutral 5600–6500K even field — shadow definition for shape only, no gel, no warm practicals in frame. COLOR LOCK Seamless-Neutral: backdrop #9A9A98±2%, skin true-to-life, wardrobe colors accurate, no gel spill — no magenta edge gradient, no orange spotlight cone. GRADE LOCK Cool-Clinical: grey axis neutral, skin neither pink nor yellow pushed, saturation -15% global — forbid magenta tint, forbid orange warmth, forbid teal skin on shadows, forbid vintage fade. Locked-off or slow linear track; no handheld. Lip-synced, delivers: "Why does water climb up a straw, dissolve salt, and stay liquid across a huge temperature range — when a similar-sized molecule would be a gas?" clear enthusiastic science communicator voice, precise but accessible diction, synthetic presenter only. Finish on presentational gesture peak, never hold dead stillness or dead frame.
shots.speech_text: Why does water climb up a straw, dissolve salt, and stay liquid across a huge temperature range — when a similar-sized molecule would be a gas?

shots.video_prompt: CONTINUITY LOCK @Julian-001: identical presenter face, smart-casual wardrobe, seamless neutral set, same eye-line to camera — seamless continuation of prior take, zero jump cut. CONTINUITY LOCK @Set-Seamless-Neutral-001: identical neutral grey seamless — same backdrop tone mid-grey #9A9A98, same floor sweep, same subject-to-backdrop distance, same falloff — seamless continuation, zero jump cut. STYLE LOCK @Style-Cool-Clinical-001: cool-clinical grade — neutral white balance, low saturation, accurate color reproduction, minimal stylistic grading, science and product trust, no warm cozy cast, no film grain. Presenter explains the phenomenon plainly, motivated gesture, clinical lighting. Camera: slow push-in medium. Neutral seamless 5500K bilateral soft key locked — symmetrical wrap, subtle nose shadow only, zero hue drift, no magenta vignette, no warm/cool lateral split. Neutral 5600–6500K even field — shadow definition for shape only, no gel, no warm practicals in frame. COLOR LOCK Seamless-Neutral: backdrop #9A9A98±2%, skin true-to-life, wardrobe colors accurate, no gel spill — no magenta edge gradient, no orange spotlight cone. GRADE LOCK Cool-Clinical: grey axis neutral, skin neither pink nor yellow pushed, saturation -15% global — forbid magenta tint, forbid orange warmth, forbid teal skin on shadows, forbid vintage fade. Locked-off or slow linear track; no handheld. Lip-synced, delivers: "Atoms with nearly equal hunger for electrons can share them instead of handing them over completely. That shared pair is a covalent bond. In water, oxygen shares with two hydrogens, but not equally — oxygen pulls harder, so the molecule is bent and polar." clear enthusiastic science communicator voice, precise but accessible diction, synthetic presenter only. Finish on presentational gesture peak, never hold dead stillness or dead frame.
shots.speech_text: Atoms with nearly equal hunger for electrons can share them instead of handing them over completely. That shared pair is a covalent bond. In water, oxygen shares with two hydrogens, but not equally — oxygen pulls harder, so the molecule is bent and polar.

shots.speech_text: We measure bond lengths and angles with spectroscopy and crystallography — then publish them in databases anyone can check. For water, the O–H length and the hundred-four-degree bend are tabulated in the NIST Chemistry WebBook, not guessed from a cartoon.

shots.speech_text: Here is the shared-pair picture: two hydrogens anchored to oxygen, electron density pulled toward oxygen's side. The cloud is exaggerated for teaching — real electrons are quantum probability, not colored balls on rails.
